voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: Madagascar — dry deciduous forest and scrubland
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: It reads scent marks on branches to gather information about other lemurs.
It hears group alarm calls and responds within seconds.
Its eyes face forward for good depth perception while leaping.
It sniffs noses with group members as a form of greeting and recognition.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives in the dry deciduous forests and scrubland of southern Madagascar.
It spends more time on the ground than almost any other lemur.
Trees provide food, sleeping sites, and escape routes from ground predators.
It ranges widely and crosses open ground to move between tree patches.
How does it send messages?: Males have scent glands on their wrists and rub them onto their striped tails.
They wave their scented tails at rivals in what is called a stink fight.
Loud group calls warn the troop of aerial predators like hawks.
A lower call alerts the troop to ground-level threats nearby.
What does it do when something changes?: It sunbathes with arms spread wide to warm up before moving.
The whole troop moves together in response to a predator alarm call.
It retreats into tree cover when it feels exposed on open ground.
Females, who lead the troop, direct when and where the group moves.
How does it learn?: Infants cling to the mother's belly and later ride on her back.
Young lemurs watch adults to learn which plants and fruits are safe to eat.
Scent-marking behavior begins at about six months of age, first by copying adults.
How does it have babies?: A female typically gives birth to one infant, sometimes twins, after about 135 days.
Infants are born with open eyes and can grip the mother within hours.
The troop protects the mother and infant during the first weeks of life.
Young lemurs are weaned at about five months and then follow the troop independently.
What are its limits?: It needs trees large enough to sleep in safely above ground predators.
Deforestation in Madagascar has removed most of its dry forest habitat.
Habitat loss has left very little suitable forest for this species.
Drought years reduce fruit and leaf availability and stress the entire troop.
What does this teach us?: A stink fight uses chemistry instead of teeth — information as a weapon.
Female leadership means group decisions are made by the most experienced members.
Its striped tail carries scent signals in its own social world.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Spends more time on the ground than almost any other lemur species.
Female-led troop social structure with matriline dominance.
Males use wrist and tail scent glands in 'stink fight' displays during mating season.
Restricted to dry deciduous forest and scrubland in southern Madagascar.
still_debated: Whether stink fights are settled ranking contests or opportunistic displays.
How much individual identity vs. group membership is coded into scent marks.
The real cognitive complexity behind troop-wide coordinated movement.
common_failure_modes: Assuming behavior seen in captivity (e.g., extended sunbathing on demand) maps 1:1 to the wild.
Confusing it with the other lemurs in our collection because 'lemur' is used loosely in public.
Treating the striped tail as camouflage rather than a scent-carrying signal.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not call the species 'common' — IUCN lists it as Endangered.
Do not describe troop decisions as male-led.
Do not equate 'stink fight' with physical combat — it is a scent display.
